hero.dek: The cold, still afternoon when the price of keeping the lights on hit £2,900 a megawatt-hour.
hero.body_md: On a cold, calm January day the wind faded to a tenth of its capacity while demand stayed high. Britain leaned on its interconnectors for close to six gigawatts of imported power — around three-fifths of everything the cables can carry — and the system price spiked to £2,900 a megawatt-hour, roughly forty times an ordinary afternoon. By the end of the day the bill for those imports came to £87.5 million, the most Britain has ever paid to import electricity in a single day. Press play and watch the day tighten.

commentary.label: 15:00 — the price spike
commentary.text: The system price reaches £2,900/MWh — about forty times a normal afternoon — as a cold, calm day meets high demand and a fading wind fleet.

commentary.label: 18:00 — peak imports
commentary.text: Net imports peak near 6,000 MW, close to three-fifths of everything the interconnectors can carry. Britain is leaning hard on its neighbours at the evening peak.

commentary.label: 21:30 — the wind bottoms out
commentary.text: Wind delivers under a tenth of its capacity — the calm that made the whole day so expensive.

hero.dek: The day a windless Britain leaned on its neighbours — until they pulled the plug.
hero.body_md: A blocking high sat over north-west Europe and the wind fell away across the whole region at once. Through the morning Britain leaned hard on imports; by the evening peak, with demand high and the wind still flat, our neighbours kept their scarce power for themselves — and the system operator paid for emergency generation to keep the lights on. Press the play button and watch the day unfold.

attributed_figures.label: reported price of evening emergency power, roughly fifteen times a normal day

commentary.label: 10:30 — peak dependence
commentary.text: Firm power is at its daily low. Net imports peak near 6,900 MW and the wind fleet is delivering about a tenth of its plate. This is the standing exposure — hours before anything goes wrong.

commentary.label: 17:30 — the price turns
commentary.text: The settled price jumps to £317/MWh as the evening peak builds and solar fades.

commentary.label: 20:00 — the emergency
commentary.text: Demand peaks. Gas has ramped to cover and imports have withdrawn as neighbours keep their power. Firm plant is now carrying the country — the ~£1,400/MWh emergency price is the market clearing that squeeze.
